name: ghl-form-css-reference
scope: GoHighLevel / HighLevel / LeadConnector inline widgets served via form_embed.js. Covers both FORM widgets (link.<sub>.com/widget/form/<id>) and CALENDAR widgets (link.<sub>.com/widget/booking/<id>). Both render inside an iframe and both expose a Custom CSS panel in the GHL builder. Selectors apply inside the rendered iframe document. Categories prefixed with `calendar-` apply only to calendar widgets; all others are form-widget selectors. The calendar's form-step screen reuses the form widget DOM, so form selectors also apply there.
stability_warning: There is no official CSS-class API from HighLevel for either widget type. Their own help doc punts to DevTools. The DOM is known to change between platform releases — every snapshot is point-in-time. For FORM widgets, the `data-q` attribute is the most stable per-field hook. For CALENDAR widgets, ARIA attributes (aria-selected, aria-pressed, aria-disabled) and substring class matching (`[class*="day-cell"]`) are the most stable hooks because GHL renames calendar classes between versions more aggressively than form classes.
how_to_use: Look up a target by `category` + `purpose`. Each entry has a `confidence` field — `confirmed` means at least one community reference has it working in production; `inferred` means it's a reasonable guess that needs DevTools verification on a live widget. For forms, prefer `data-q` field targeting over positional selectors. For calendars, prefer ARIA attributes + substring class matching over exact class names.

sources.name: krishnalewin-hash/tourism-ui-kit
sources.notes: Most actively maintained community reference for inline GHL form CSS. Sibling repo: krishnalewin-hash/airport-transfer-form. Battle-tested against the live LeadConnector iframe DOM.

sources.name: Resonant Studios — Coordinator Combo Lead Magnet (live form #HT93KLrv082pr6Wvq9rL)
sources.notes: Empirical findings from styling a live newer-builder GHL form. Confirms that the v1 .ghl-btn.ghl-submit-btn selector did not match this form's button, and that GHL exposes its theme colour via CSS custom properties on :root that must be hijacked to win the cascade.

sources.name: simple10 GHL survey CSS gist
sources.notes: Survey-specific (multi-step). Confirms the `_builder-form` id, the `.ghl-footer` + `.ghl-progress-bar*` family, and the `.hl-app` namespace.

sources.name: Apply Custom CSS to Forms/Surveys/Quizzes
sources.notes: Officially confirms only generic selectors (`input`, `textarea`, `button[type="submit"]`, `label`, `.hl-error`, `.error-message`). Explicitly tells users to use right-click → Inspect for anything specific.

sources.name: HighLevel Ideas — custom CSS classes on form fields
sources.notes: Open feature request for user-assignable classes on form fields. Years-old, never shipped. Confirms there is no per-field class API today.

sources.name: Squarespace forum — GHL form button CSS
sources.notes: Anecdotal colour overrides on submit buttons. No new selectors beyond what's in tourism-ui-kit / simple10.

sources.name: erobrien/mwcv3 — ghl-calendar-overrides.css
sources.notes: Best community reference for GHL CALENDAR widget custom CSS. Header comment warns 'GHL renames classes between versions' and uses substring matching (`[class*="day-cell"]`) rather than exact class names. Calendar-equivalent of tourism-ui-kit's role for forms.

sources.name: Calendar Widget Customization — HighLevel Support
sources.notes: Confirms Custom CSS panel exists for Neo-style calendars. Says 'Primary Color impacts buttons, dates, time slots' (implying calendar consumes the same --primary root variable as forms). Lists zero specific selectors. Punts to DevTools.

sources.name: HighLevel Ideas — Add Custom CSS styling to Calendars
sources.notes: Confirms there is no first-class CSS class API for calendar styling — the Custom CSS panel is currently the only path.

categories.structure: The form's root element + namespace wrappers.
categories.field-wrapper: Containers that wrap a single field (label + input + error).
categories.field-targeting: How to target a specific field by name. Use `data-q` whenever possible.
categories.field-types: Selectors that vary by field type (checkbox, radio, dropdown, etc.).
categories.labels: Field labels and the required-asterisk markup.
categories.validation: Error states on fields + error message text.
categories.submit: The submit button and its container.
categories.links: Anchor tags inside form copy (terms-and-conditions, privacy links).
categories.survey: Selectors specific to multi-step survey/quiz forms.
categories.host-page: Selectors on the parent page where the iframe is embedded.
categories.theme-variables: GHL's own CSS custom properties — override these at :root to re-skin the form's default theme colour at the source instead of fighting it per-element.
categories.calendar-month-grid: CALENDAR widget — month header, day-of-week column headers, navigation arrows.
categories.calendar-date-tile: CALENDAR widget — individual date cells + their states (selected, today, disabled, outside-current-month).
categories.calendar-time-slot: CALENDAR widget — time-slot buttons + their states (selected, disabled).
categories.calendar-cta: CALENDAR widget — confirm/book/continue button that advances to the next step.
categories.calendar-form-step: CALENDAR widget — the form-fields screen on the calendar's final step. Reuses the form widget DOM; the standard form selectors (form#_builder-form, .ghl-question, .form-field-wrapper, data-q) apply here.

selectors.purpose: Root <form> element inside the iframe document. Use this as the scoping root for all form-level overrides.
Top-level HighLevel app namespace wrapper. Useful for high-specificity overrides like `.hl-app .form-control`.
Container that wraps a single question (label + input + helper text + error).
Bootstrap-style row that contains one or more field wrappers — controls side-by-side field layouts.
Per-field wrapper. Often combined with a Bootstrap col class (e.g. `.col-12.form-field-wrapper` for full-width, `.col-6.form-field-wrapper` for half).
Target a specific field by its internal name. MOST STABLE per-field hook. Replace <field_key> with the field's internal name set in the form builder.
selectors.notes: Prefer this over :nth-child or positional selectors. data-q mirrors the form builder's internal key and survives most DOM refactors.

selectors.purpose: Target the wrapper of a specific field by its internal name. Useful for hiding rows (hidden fields, conditional fields).
selectors.notes: Used in tourism-ui-kit to hide rows containing hidden inputs. Requires :has() — supported in all modern browsers as of 2024.

selectors.purpose: Bootstrap-ish base class on most inputs/selects/textareas. Useful for general typographic + border overrides.
selectors.notes: Also addressable via `.hl-app .form-control` for higher specificity.

selectors.purpose: Vue-powered custom dropdown (used for select fields with rich options).

selectors.selector: input[type="text"], input[type="email"], input[type="tel"], input[type="number"], input[type="url"], input[type="password"], input[type="date"], input[type="search"]
selectors.purpose: Standard text-style HTML inputs. Group target for type/colour/border/radius overrides.
Native checkbox. Use `accent-color` to brand it without rebuilding the control.
Native radio. Use `accent-color` to brand it.
Field label. Sits above the input by default.
Required-field asterisk. Exact markup varies — most common is `<sup>*` appended to the label, but `<span class="required">` is also seen.
selectors.notes: Not confirmed by any community reference. Verify in DevTools when building a new form. The default GHL styling is a red `*` next to the label text.

selectors.purpose: Error state class added to invalid inputs.
Error message text that appears below an invalid field.
selectors.notes: Multiple class names seen across versions — target all four for safety.

selectors.purpose: The form's submit button. `.ghl-btn.ghl-submit-btn` is the official class pair; `button[type="submit"]` is the safe fallback.

selectors.selector: body button, body button[type="submit"], body input[type="submit"], body .ghl-btn, body .ghl-submit-btn, body button[class*="submit"], body button[class*="primary"], body button[class*="btn"], body [role="button"], body form button
selectors.purpose: Broadest-possible submit-button target. Use when the form is rendered by the newer GHL form builder and the standard .ghl-btn.ghl-submit-btn class doesn't match. Empirically required for forms where the v1 selector leaves the button styled in GHL's default theme blue.
selectors.notes: Body-prefix raises specificity over GHL's stylesheet. Also explicitly override background-color AND background-image so the gradient and the flat-colour fallback both get blanked.

selectors.selector: body button > *, body button[type="submit"] > *, body .ghl-btn > *, body .ghl-submit-btn > *
selectors.purpose: Some GHL buttons wrap the label in a child <span> that carries its own colour, which beats the parent button's colour. Force every descendant to inherit colour + transparent background.

GHL's form builder applies the form-level theme colour through CSS custom properties on :root. Names vary by builder version. Hijack all of them at :root with !important to re-skin the form's default theme blue at the source — much more reliable than fighting each element's `color: var(--primary)` rule downstream.
selectors.notes: Not all of these variables will exist on any given form — defining a variable that isn't referenced is a no-op, so the over-coverage is safe.

selectors.purpose: Terms-and-conditions consent block wrapper (checkbox + legal text + link). Class names vary across builder versions, so the `:has(input[type="checkbox"])` form is the most reliable hook.
selectors.notes: Empirically verified against a live form. The :has() form catches every variant of the wrapper regardless of class.

selectors.selector: .terms-conditions *, .terms-and-conditions *, .consent-text *, label:has(input[type="checkbox"]) *, .form-field-wrapper:has(input[type="checkbox"]) *:not(input):not(svg):not(a), input[type="checkbox"] ~ label, input[type="checkbox"] ~ span, input[type="checkbox"] ~ div, input[type="checkbox"] + span, input[type="checkbox"] + div
selectors.purpose: Descendant text nodes inside a terms-and-conditions block. GHL sets `color` directly on the inner <span>/<div>, which beats inheritance from the wrapper — so styling the wrapper alone leaves the text rendering in whatever GHL's default is (usually ink-faint, which is invisible on a dark surface).
selectors.notes: Pair this with a wrapper-level rule. The :not(a) escape preserves link colour inside the block — re-apply the link rule separately afterwards.

selectors.selector: input::placeholder, textarea::placeholder, input::-webkit-input-placeholder, textarea::-webkit-input-placeholder, input::-moz-placeholder, textarea::-moz-placeholder, input:-ms-input-placeholder, textarea:-ms-input-placeholder, input::-ms-input-placeholder, textarea::-ms-input-placeholder
selectors.purpose: Placeholder text colour. Vendor-prefixed selectors are required because GHL's stylesheet uses `color: var(--primary)` on placeholders — if you hijack --primary to your brand colour at :root (which you should), placeholders inherit the brand colour and stop reading as 'helper text'. Explicit overrides with all five prefixes force them back to ink-soft / muted grey.
selectors.notes: Also set `-webkit-text-fill-color` on the same selectors — some webkit builds use the fill colour over the regular colour for placeholder rendering.

selectors.purpose: Anchor tags inside the form (terms-and-conditions, privacy policy, etc).
Survey question set wrapper (multi-step forms only).
Survey footer area containing the navigation buttons.
Survey 'Next' button (advances to the next step).
Survey 'Back' button (returns to the previous step).
Survey progress bar (track + fill + label text).
Post-submission confirmation screen.
The placeholder div + the actual iframe written by form_embed.js. Lives on the PARENT page, not inside the iframe. Use to size or constrain the embed externally.
selectors.notes: Placeholder id pattern: `inline-<formId>`. Iframe id is appended by the loader.

selectors.purpose: Root container for the GHL calendar widget. Multiple selectors because GHL renames the wrapper class between Classic and Neo calendar styles and across builder versions.
selectors.notes: Use substring matching as primary. erobrien's file uses all three of these as fallbacks.

selectors.purpose: The 'November 2026' style header that labels the current month/year.
Previous / next month navigation buttons.
selectors.notes: Substring matching survives GHL's version churn better than exact classes.

selectors.purpose: The Mon/Tue/Wed/... column headers above the date grid.
Individual date tile in the month grid. Excludes weekday-header cells via :not().
selectors.notes: The :not([class*="weekday"]) guard prevents this rule from accidentally hitting the column headers.

selectors.purpose: Currently-selected date. ARIA attributes are the MOST stable hook here — they're semantic and survive GHL's class renames.
selectors.notes: Prefer aria-pressed="true" and aria-selected="true" over class-based selectors. The [class*="selected"] is a class-name fallback in case ARIA isn't applied.

selectors.purpose: Today's date when it's NOT also selected. The :not() guard prevents this rule from overriding the selected-state rule when today is the selected date.
Date that's not bookable (past, fully booked, outside availability window).
selectors.notes: aria-disabled="true" is the most stable signal — semantic and version-resistant.

selectors.purpose: Greyed-out 'spillover' date from the previous or next month, when the grid shows 6 rows.
selectors.notes: Class names vary heavily — three different names seen across versions.

selectors.purpose: Individual time-slot button shown after a date is selected.
Currently-selected time slot. ARIA attributes preferred.
Disabled time slot — past, booked-out, or unavailable.
Timezone dropdown that lets visitors switch the displayed slot times.
Confirm / book / continue button that advances the calendar through its steps. Inferred from form-widget naming convention.
selectors.notes: button[type="submit"] is the safest single fallback.

selectors.purpose: The calendar's form-step screen (where visitor enters name/email/phone before confirming) reuses the form widget DOM. All standard form selectors apply on that step.
selectors.notes: Means you can paste your form CSS rules into the calendar Custom CSS and they'll style the form step automatically.

selectors.purpose: Post-booking confirmation screen with the 'You're booked for...' message.

anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: Order-dependent. Breaks the moment a field is added, removed, or reordered in the form builder. The simple10 gist uses this and explicitly marks it as brittle.

anti_patterns.pattern: Targeting field types by guessing the wrapper class
anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: GHL has no per-type wrapper class — the wrapper is always `.form-field-wrapper` regardless of whether the input is text, checkbox, radio, signature, or file.
anti_patterns.use_instead: Match on the inner input's type attribute or its data-q name.

anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: GHL's own stylesheet loads after the Custom CSS panel. Without !important, almost every rule loses the cascade.
anti_patterns.use_instead: Use !important on every override rule. Standard practice for these embeds.

anti_patterns.pattern: Only targeting .ghl-btn.ghl-submit-btn for the submit button
anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: Newer-version forms (2026+) often render submit buttons without the .ghl-btn / .ghl-submit-btn classes. The button keeps GHL's default theme blue because the override never matches.
anti_patterns.use_instead: Use the broad submit-button-broad selector + the theme-variables :root override together. Belt-and-braces.

anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: GHL applies the theme colour as `background-color` and sometimes adds a `background-image` gradient. The shorthand `background` should reset both, but inline `background-image` styles can survive — set them explicitly to `none`.
anti_patterns.use_instead: Set all three: background, background-color, AND background-image.

anti_patterns.pattern: Styling only the wrapper for a multi-text block (terms-and-conditions, helper text)
anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: CSS `color` is inheritable, but GHL frequently sets `color` directly on the inner <span>/<div>, which beats inheritance from the wrapper. Styling only the wrapper leaves the inner text rendering in GHL's default (often ink-faint, invisible on dark surfaces).
anti_patterns.use_instead: Style the wrapper AND every descendant: `.wrapper *:not(input):not(svg):not(a) { color: ... }`. Then re-apply link colour separately.

anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: If you hijack --primary at :root (to override the theme button colour), placeholders inherit it via GHL's `color: var(--primary)` rule on ::placeholder. Without vendor-prefixed overrides (-webkit, -moz, -ms), placeholders render in your brand colour and stop reading as helper text.
anti_patterns.use_instead: Set all five: ::placeholder, ::-webkit-input-placeholder, ::-moz-placeholder, :-ms-input-placeholder, ::-ms-input-placeholder. Also -webkit-text-fill-color for webkit edge cases.

anti_patterns.pattern: Box-shadow on the form card when html/body is painted with the same card colour
anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: If you paint the iframe's html/body with --rs-card-bg to hide the default white iframe-body leak (a common technique), any box-shadow on the <form> projects onto an identical-colour surface — and reads as a visible darker stripe directly below the card. Particularly bad in dark mode where shadow alphas are 4-5× heavier than light mode. Light mode often looks fine and disguises the bug.
anti_patterns.use_instead: Drop box-shadow on the form card entirely. The 1 px border + border-radius define the card edge without needing a shadow. Or, if you want shadow, paint html/body with --bg-sunken (page section colour) instead so the shadow has a contrasting surface to land on.

anti_patterns.pattern: CALENDAR — Targeting exact class names instead of substring + ARIA
anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: GHL renames calendar classes between releases more aggressively than form classes. Exact-match selectors like `.day-cell` or `.time-slot-button` break the moment GHL ships a new version. erobrien's reference file explicitly flags this and uses substring matching throughout.
anti_patterns.use_instead: Always pair substring class matching with ARIA fallbacks: `[class*="day-cell"][aria-pressed="true"]`. The ARIA attribute is semantic and survives DOM refactors; the substring class match handles cases where ARIA isn't applied.

anti_patterns.pattern: CALENDAR — Hitting weekday-header cells with the date-tile rule
anti_patterns.why_to_avoid: Naive selectors like `button[class*="day"]` match BOTH the date tiles AND the Mon/Tue/Wed column headers, because both have 'day' in their class name. The header styling then gets stomped by the date-tile styling.
anti_patterns.use_instead: Add a :not() guard: `button[class*="day"]:not([class*="weekday"]):not([class*="day-name"])`. Style the column headers in a separate, earlier rule so the date-tile rule doesn't fight it.
